nativeRange.habitat: Lives partially buried in sand or rubble between coral heads and along rocky reef edges and slopes, typically in areas with moderate to strong currents; oral disc and tentacles exposed at the substrate surface

decorPreferences: deep sand or fine rubble bed (minimum 10 cm / 4 inches)
open substrate area between rockwork for column burial
stable rock or rubble for column anchoring
room for the oral disc to expand fully without contacting corals

diet.notes: Photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiotes (primary energy source). Target-feeding is generally advised one to two times per week with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, krill, chopped silversides, small pieces of fish, or shrimp. Feed directly to the oral disc using tongs or a feeding stick; pieces should be small enough that the anemone closes around them without regurgitating.

goodWith: Amphiprion akindynos (Barrier Reef anemonefish — documented host)
non-aggressive reef fish kept clear of the oral disc
avoidWith: other anemone species placed within reach (sting and chemical aggression)
LPS and SPS corals within the wandering range of the column
small ornamental shrimp and crabs that may stray onto tentacles
angelfish and butterflyfish known to nip anemones
unguarded powerheads, return pumps, and overflow intakes

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5136x5136. Tack-sharp in-situ shot, whole animal centered; beaded tentacles with pale/white tips clearly visible, vibrant magenta-and-tan coloration — textbook diagnostic ID.
Jenny (JennyHuang) from Taipei, 2933x2163. Sharp, vibrant golden individual with Clark's clownfish and porcelain crab; clearly shows beaded tentacles and the host relationship.
Bernard DUPONT from France, 4416x2649. Sharp whole-animal shot on sand showing the sand-dweller habit; beaded tentacle morphology plainly visible.

summary: The Aurora Anemone (Heteractis aurora) is an Indo-Pacific host anemone recognized by its distinctive beaded tentacles. It buries its column in a deep sand bed, needs bright reef lighting and moderate-to-strong flow, and is a documented host for seven clownfish species. Intermediate care is generally advised.
careNotes:
Identification: The Aurora Anemone is best identified by the distinctive 'beaded' tentacles — each tapered tentacle bears up to about 20 small swellings down its length, giving the appearance of beads on a string, with magenta or white tips. The flattened oral disc can reach around 25 cm (10 in) across in mature specimens; tentacles tend to remain relatively short (around 5 cm).

Taxonomy note: Hobby and trade sources commonly place Heteractis in the family Stichodactylidae (the carpet-anemone family). Some recent revisions place Heteractis aurora in a separate family, Heteractidae. Both placements appear in active use across the scientific and hobbyist literature.

Substrate requirement: Aurora Anemones are sand-bed dwellers. The column is typically buried in sand or fine rubble between coral heads, with only the oral disc and tentacles exposed at the surface. A minimum of about 10 cm (4 in) of sand or rubble is generally advised; without a suitable substrate the anemone tends to wander in search of a place to burrow, which exposes it to powerheads and to corals it can sting on contact.

Lighting and flow: Bright reef lighting and moderate-to-strong flow generally suit this species, consistent with its natural habitat on current-swept reef slopes. PAR in roughly the 150–300 range at sand level is a reasonable working target; lower light can be tolerated but tends to limit zooxanthellae activity and may force more aggressive supplemental feeding.

The Aurora Anemone is described as more of a 'nursery anemone' in the wild: juvenile clownfish are common associates, while sexually mature individuals are rarely hosted. Commensal porcelain crabs (Neopetrolisthes) and Periclimenes anemone shrimp are also commonly associated.

Aggression: Like other host anemones, the Aurora has a potent sting capable of injuring or killing small fish and invertebrates that contact the tentacles, and can chemically antagonize nearby corals. Leaving 20–30 cm of clearance around its likely wander range is generally advised, as any coral within reach is at risk.

Medications: Anemones do not tolerate copper-based medications well. Treat any quarantine or display dosing decisions with this in mind.
breedingNotes: Captive reproduction of Aurora Anemones has not been documented as a reproducible aquarium practice. Anemones in this genus can reproduce asexually by fission under favorable conditions, but this tends to occur spontaneously rather than being induced by hobbyists. Sexual reproduction in the wild involves broadcast spawning.

primary.name: Reeflings — Beaded sea anemone (Heteractis aurora)
primary.notes: Used as primary because LiveAquaria product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/beaded-sea-anemone) returned HTTP 404 at access time. Provided water parameters (22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026), minimum tank (200 L), temperament (semi-aggressive), care level (moderate-easy), diet (carnivore), depth (intertidal to ~20 m), family placement in Stichodactylidae, and host clownfish reference (A. clarkii, A. chrysopterus).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Heteractis aurora
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy and ecology. Lists family Heteractidae (alternative placement to Stichodactylidae — both appear in active use). Provided the canonical list of seven clownfish hosts (A. akindynos, A. allardi, A. bicinctus, A. clarkii, A. chrysogaster, A. chrysopterus, A. tricinctus), the 'nursery anemone' ecological role, oral disc width up to ~25 cm, beaded tentacle morphology (up to 20 swellings per tentacle), native range (Micronesia, Melanesia, East Africa, Red Sea, Australia, Ryukyu Islands), and commensal associations with Periclimenes shrimp, porcelain crabs, and Dascyllus trimaculatus. Not used as a primary husbandry source.

additional.name: Quality Marine — Colored Beaded Anemone (Heteractis aurora)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12) — all agreed with primary except a tighter SG ceiling (1.025 vs Reeflings' 1.026). Rated care level 'Difficult' and temperament 'Aggressive' (more conservative than Reeflings' 'Moderate to easy / semi-aggressive') — middle position (intermediate / semi-aggressive) adopted. Reports maximum diameter around 10 in (~25 cm) and notes copper sensitivity.

additional.name: CoralRealm — Sea Anemone Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (75 gal / ~284 L minimum with at least 4 in of substrate) and water parameters (72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12 — agreed with Quality Marine). Adult diameter listed as 10 in (~25 cm), consistent with Wikipedia's oral-disc figure. Confirmed preference for strong lighting and moderate-to-strong flow.
